[[Category:Kazusa Province]] [[Category:Kuni no Miyatsuko]] [[Category:Chiba Prefecture history]] [[Category:Japanese historical titles]]

{{Infobox historical administrative title | name = Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko (Shang-Kamitsu-Kamiko) | native_name = дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ | period = [[Kofun period|3rd to 6th centuries]] | region = [[Kazusa Province]] ([[Chiba Prefecture]]) | governing_body = [[Kuni no Miyatsuko|Regional chief]] under the [[Yamato court]] }}
'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' ({{ill|Shang-Kamitsu-Kamiko|en|дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }}, lit. "Upper Kamitsu Country Chief"), also known as '''Kamitsu Unakami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko''', was a [[Kuni no Miyatsuko|regional ruler]] of the [[Kazusa Province|Kazusa region]] (modern-day {{ill|Ichihara, Chiba|en|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ}}) in ancient Japan. The title "Kamitsu Unakami" refers to governance over the upper reaches of the [[Unegawa|Unakami River basin]].
== Overview == The territory governed by the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko included areas along the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|en|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin. Archaeological finds from the area suggest early ties to the [[Yamato court]]: * The {{ill|Kamito Kofun Group|en|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} includes [[Makimuku-style tumuli|Makimuku-type tumuli]] dating to the {{ill|3rd century|en|3rd century CE}}, possibly early [[kofun]] or {{ill|Yayoi burial mounds|en|ејҘз”ҹеўідёҳеў“}}. * The {{ill|Inaridai Tomb No. 1|en|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі}} yielded a famous [[iron sword inscription|inscribed iron sword]] bearing the {{ill|King's Reward Inscription|en|зҺӢиіңйҠҳ}}.
The proximity of large-scale tumuli such as the {{ill|Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|en|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} further reinforces the region's prominence as a political center during the Kofun period. An early connection with maritime trade is suggested by a poem in the {{ill|Man'yЕҚshЕ«|en|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}, which describes the Kamitsu Unakami lagoon as a vital point for {{ill|waterborne transport|en|ж°ҙдёҠдәӨйҖҡ}}.
Scholars hypothesize that the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko initially governed a broad swath of central {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|еҚғи‘үзңҢ}}, extending into parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|en|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}. The later establishment of neighboring polities such as {{ill|Musha Kuni|en|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪ}} likely reflects administrative reorganization by the Yamato court.
During the {{ill|5th century|en|5th century CE}}, the Kamitsu Unakami polity is thought to have been subdivided: 1. The emergence of {{ill|Shimo Unakami Kuni|en|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} in the southern basin. 2. Competition from other rising local powers, such as {{ill|Kikuma Kuni|en|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }}, weakened Kamitsu Unakami's influence.
Evidence of this decline is seen in the {{ill|Anesaki Kofun Group|en|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}. The construction of large tumuli, such as the 130-meter {{ill|Anesaki Tenjinyama Kofun|en|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late {{ill|4th century|en|4th century CE}}) and the 103-meter {{ill|Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|en|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (late {{ill|5th century|en|5th century CE}}), shows early prosperity. However, tumuli from later periods diminish in scale, underscoring the polity's waning power.
== Clan Lineage == According to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}, the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko traced its origins to {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|en|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}, a son of {{ill|Amaterasu|en|еӨ©з…§еӨ§зҘһ}}. Specifically: * Founder: {{ill|Takerihatori-no-Mikoto|en|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ}}, a descendant of Ame-no-Hohi.
The {{ill|Kuni no Miyatsuko Registry|en|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}} states that during Emperor {{ill|Seimu|en|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, {{ill|Ninotarikahatahi-no-Mikoto|en|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, a seventh-generation descendant, was appointed the first Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atae|en|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the first ruler of {{ill|Shimo Unakami Kuni|en|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|en|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}.
The Kamitsu Unakami lineage shares ties with prominent clans, including the {{ill|Izumo-no-Kami Clan|en|еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ }} and the {{ill|Musashi-no-Kami Clan|en|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}. Notably, the {{ill|Shang-Kazusa Clan|en|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}, mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}, likely descends from the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko.
== Associated Shrines == The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|en|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ}}, dedicated to regional deities, is linked to the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. The shrine enshrines: * {{ill|Ninotarikahatahi-no-Mikoto|en|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, the first Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * Other local deities tied to the {{ill|Anesaki Kofun Group|en|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Kokushi Daijiten'' ({{ill|Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan|en|еҗүе·қејҳж–ҮйӨЁ}}). * Sakamoto TarЕҚ and Hirano Kunio. ''Nihon Kodai Shizoku Jinmei Jiten.'' Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan. * HЕҚga Toshio. ''Koshi and Izumo no Yoake.'' HЕҚrei Shuppan, 2008.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kuni no Miyatsuko|en|еӣҪйҖ гҒ®дёҖиҰ§}}

[[Category:Kazusa Province]] [[Category:Kuni no Miyatsuko]] [[Category:Chiba Prefecture history]] [[Category:Japanese historical titles]] [[Category:Https://ja.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=]][[ja:%E4%B8%8A%E6%B5%B7%E4%B8%8A%E5%9B%BD%E9%80%A0]][[category:&action=edit]]

{{Infobox historical administrative title | name = Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko (Shang-Kamitsu-Kamiko) | native_name = дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ | period = [[Kofun period|3rd to 6th centuries]] | region = [[Kazusa Province]] ([[Chiba Prefecture]]) | governing_body = [[Kuni no Miyatsuko|Regional chief]] under the [[Yamato court]] }}
'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' ({{ill|Shang-Kamitsu-Kamiko|en|дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }}, lit. "Upper Kamitsu Country Chief"), also known as '''Kamitsu Unakami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko''', was a [[Kuni no Miyatsuko|regional ruler]] of the [[Kazusa Province|Kazusa region]] (modern-day {{ill|Ichihara, Chiba|en|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ}}) in ancient Japan. The title "Kamitsu Unakami" refers to governance over the upper reaches of the [[Unegawa|Unakami River basin]].
== Overview == The territory governed by the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko included areas along the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|en|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin. Archaeological finds from the area suggest early ties to the [[Yamato court]]: * The {{ill|Kamito Kofun Group|en|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} includes [[Makimuku-style tumuli|Makimuku-type tumuli]] dating to the {{ill|3rd century|en|3rd century CE}}, possibly early [[kofun]] or {{ill|Yayoi burial mounds|en|ејҘз”ҹеўідёҳеў“}}. * The {{ill|Inaridai Tomb No. 1|en|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі}} yielded a famous [[iron sword inscription|inscribed iron sword]] bearing the {{ill|King's Reward Inscription|en|зҺӢиіңйҠҳ}}.
The proximity of large-scale tumuli such as the {{ill|Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|en|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} further reinforces the region's prominence as a political center during the Kofun period. An early connection with maritime trade is suggested by a poem in the {{ill|Man'yЕҚshЕ«|en|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}, which describes the Kamitsu Unakami lagoon as a vital point for {{ill|waterborne transport|en|ж°ҙдёҠдәӨйҖҡ}}.
Scholars hypothesize that the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko initially governed a broad swath of central {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|еҚғи‘үзңҢ}}, extending into parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|en|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}. The later establishment of neighboring polities such as {{ill|Musha Kuni|en|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪ}} likely reflects administrative reorganization by the Yamato court.
During the {{ill|5th century|en|5th century CE}}, the Kamitsu Unakami polity is thought to have been subdivided: 1. The emergence of {{ill|Shimo Unakami Kuni|en|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} in the southern basin. 2. Competition from other rising local powers, such as {{ill|Kikuma Kuni|en|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }}, weakened Kamitsu Unakami's influence.
Evidence of this decline is seen in the {{ill|Anesaki Kofun Group|en|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}. The construction of large tumuli, such as the 130-meter {{ill|Anesaki Tenjinyama Kofun|en|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late {{ill|4th century|en|4th century CE}}) and the 103-meter {{ill|Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|en|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (late {{ill|5th century|en|5th century CE}}), shows early prosperity. However, tumuli from later periods diminish in scale, underscoring the polity's waning power.
== Clan Lineage == According to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}, the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko traced its origins to {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|en|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}, a son of {{ill|Amaterasu|en|еӨ©з…§еӨ§зҘһ}}. Specifically: * Founder: {{ill|Takerihatori-no-Mikoto|en|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ}}, a descendant of Ame-no-Hohi.
The {{ill|Kuni no Miyatsuko Registry|en|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}} states that during Emperor {{ill|Seimu|en|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, {{ill|Ninotarikahatahi-no-Mikoto|en|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, a seventh-generation descendant, was appointed the first Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atae|en|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the first ruler of {{ill|Shimo Unakami Kuni|en|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|en|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}.
The Kamitsu Unakami lineage shares ties with prominent clans, including the {{ill|Izumo-no-Kami Clan|en|еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ }} and the {{ill|Musashi-no-Kami Clan|en|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}. Notably, the {{ill|Shang-Kazusa Clan|en|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}, mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}, likely descends from the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko.
== Associated Shrines == The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|en|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ}}, dedicated to regional deities, is linked to the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. The shrine enshrines: * {{ill|Ninotarikahatahi-no-Mikoto|en|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, the first Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * Other local deities tied to the {{ill|Anesaki Kofun Group|en|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Kokushi Daijiten'' ({{ill|Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan|en|еҗүе·қејҳж–ҮйӨЁ}}). * Sakamoto TarЕҚ and Hirano Kunio. ''Nihon Kodai Shizoku Jinmei Jiten.'' Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan. * HЕҚga Toshio. ''Koshi and Izumo no Yoake.'' HЕҚrei Shuppan, 2008.

An early connection with maritime trade is suggested by a poem in the {{ill|Man'yЕҚshЕ«|en|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}, which describes the Kamitsu Unakami lagoon as a vital point for {{ill|waterborne transport|en|ж°ҙдёҠдәӨйҖҡ}}.
Scholars hypothesize that the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko initially governed a broad swath of central {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|еҚғи‘үзңҢ}}, extending into parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|en|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}. The later establishment of neighboring polities such as {{ill|Musha Kuni|en|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪ}} likely reflects administrative reorganization by the Yamato court.
During the {{ill|5th century|en|5th century CE}}, the Kamitsu Unakami polity is thought to have been subdivided: 1. The emergence of {{ill|Shimo Unakami Kuni|en|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} in the southern basin. 2. Competition from other rising local powers, such as {{ill|Kikuma Kuni|en|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }}, weakened Kamitsu Unakami's influence.
Evidence of this decline is seen in the {{ill|Anesaki Kofun Group|en|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}. The construction of large tumuli, such as the 130-meter {{ill|Anesaki Tenjinyama Kofun|en|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late {{ill|4th century|en|4th century CE}}) and the 103-meter {{ill|Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|en|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (late {{ill|5th century|en|5th century CE}}), shows early prosperity. However, tumuli from later periods diminish in scale, underscoring the polity's waning power.
== Clan Lineage == According to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}, the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko traced its origins to {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|en|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}, a son of {{ill|Amaterasu|en|еӨ©з…§еӨ§зҘһ}}. Specifically: * Founder: {{ill|Takerihatori-no-Mikoto|en|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ}}, a descendant of Ame-no-Hohi.
The {{ill|Kuni no Miyatsuko Registry|en|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}} states that during Emperor {{ill|Seimu|en|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, {{ill|Ninotarikahatahi-no-Mikoto|en|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, a seventh-generation descendant, was appointed the first Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atae|en|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the first ruler of {{ill|Shimo Unakami Kuni|en|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|en|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}.
The Kamitsu Unakami lineage shares ties with prominent clans, including the {{ill|Izumo-no-Kami Clan|en|еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ }} and the {{ill|Musashi-no-Kami Clan|en|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}. Notably, the {{ill|Shang-Kazusa Clan|en|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}, mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}, likely descends from the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko.
== Associated Shrines == The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|en|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ}}, dedicated to regional deities, is linked to the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. The shrine enshrines: * {{ill|Ninotarikahatahi-no-Mikoto|en|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, the first Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * Other local deities tied to the {{ill|Anesaki Kofun Group|en|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}

The title "Kamitsu Unakami" refers to governance over the upper reaches of the [[Unegawa|Unakami River basin]].

Evidence of this decline is seen in the {{ill|Anesaki Kofun Group|en|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}. The construction of large tumuli, such as the 130-meter {{ill|Anesaki Tenjinyama Kofun|en|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late {{ill|4th century|en|4th century CE}}) and the 103-meter {{ill|Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|en|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (late {{ill|5th century|en|5th century CE}}), shows early prosperity. However, tumuli from later periods diminish in scale, underscoring the polity's waning power.
== Clan Lineage == According to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}, the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko traced its origins to {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|en|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}, a son of {{ill|Amaterasu|en|еӨ©з…§еӨ§зҘһ}}. Specifically: * Founder: {{ill|Takerihatori-no-Mikoto|en|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ}}, a descendant of Ame-no-Hohi.
The {{ill|Kuni no Miyatsuko Registry|en|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}} states that during Emperor {{ill|Seimu|en|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, {{ill|Ninotarikahatahi-no-Mikoto|en|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, a seventh-generation descendant, was appointed the first Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atae|en|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the first ruler of {{ill|Shimo Unakami Kuni|en|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|en|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}.
The Kamitsu Unakami lineage shares ties with prominent clans, including the {{ill|Izumo-no-Kami Clan|en|еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ }} and the {{ill|Musashi-no-Kami Clan|en|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}. Notably, the {{ill|Shang-Kazusa Clan|en|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}, mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}, likely descends from the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko.
== Associated Shrines == The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|en|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ}}, dedicated to regional deities, is linked to the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. The shrine enshrines: * {{ill|Ninotarikahatahi-no-Mikoto|en|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, the first Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * Other local deities tied to the {{ill|Anesaki Kofun Group|en|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}.

{{Infobox historical administrative title | name = Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko (Shang-Kamitsu-Kamiko) | native_name = дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ | period = [[Kofun period|3rd to 6th centuries]] | region = [[Kazusa Province]] ([[Chiba Prefecture]]) | governing_body = [[Kuni no Miyatsuko|Regional chief]] under the [[Yamato court]] }}
'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' ({{ill|Shang-Kamitsu-Kamiko|en|дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }}, lit. "Upper Kamitsu Country Chief"), also known as '''Kamitsu Unakami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko''', was a [[Kuni no Miyatsuko|regional ruler]] of the [[Kazusa Province|Kazusa region]] (modern-day {{ill|Ichihara, Chiba|en|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ}}) in ancient Japan. The title "Kamitsu Unakami" refers to governance over the upper reaches of the [[Unegawa|Unakami River basin]].
== Overview == The territory governed by the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko included areas along the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|en|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin. Archaeological finds from the area suggest early ties to the [[Yamato court]]: * The {{ill|Kamito Kofun Group|en|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} includes [[Makimuku-style tumuli|Makimuku-type tumuli]] dating to the {{ill|3rd century|en|3rd century CE}}, possibly early [[kofun]] or {{ill|Yayoi burial mounds|en|ејҘз”ҹеўідёҳеў“}}. * The {{ill|Inaridai Tomb No. 1|en|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі}} yielded a famous [[iron sword inscription|inscribed iron sword]] bearing the {{ill|King's Reward Inscription|en|зҺӢиіңйҠҳ}}.
The proximity of large-scale tumuli such as the {{ill|Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|en|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} further reinforces the region's prominence as a political center during the Kofun period. An early connection with maritime trade is suggested by a poem in the {{ill|Man'yЕҚshЕ«|en|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}, which describes the Kamitsu Unakami lagoon as a vital point for {{ill|waterborne transport|en|ж°ҙдёҠдәӨйҖҡ}}.
Scholars hypothesize that the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko initially governed a broad swath of central {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|еҚғи‘үзңҢ}}, extending into parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|en|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}. The later establishment of neighboring polities such as {{ill|Musha Kuni|en|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪ}} likely reflects administrative reorganization by the Yamato court.
During the {{ill|5th century|en|5th century CE}}, the Kamitsu Unakami polity is thought to have been subdivided: 1. The emergence of {{ill|Shimo Unakami Kuni|en|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} in the southern basin. 2. Competition from other rising local powers, such as {{ill|Kikuma Kuni|en|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }}, weakened Kamitsu Unakami's influence.
Evidence of this decline is seen in the {{ill|Anesaki Kofun Group|en|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}. The construction of large tumuli, such as the 130-meter {{ill|Anesaki Tenjinyama Kofun|en|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late {{ill|4th century|en|4th century CE}}) and the 103-meter {{ill|Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|en|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (late {{ill|5th century|en|5th century CE}}), shows early prosperity. However, tumuli from later periods diminish in scale, underscoring the polity's waning power.
== Clan Lineage == According to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}, the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko traced its origins to {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|en|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}, a son of {{ill|Amaterasu|en|еӨ©з…§еӨ§зҘһ}}. Specifically: * Founder: {{ill|Takerihatori-no-Mikoto|en|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ}}, a descendant of Ame-no-Hohi.
The {{ill|Kuni no Miyatsuko Registry|en|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}} states that during Emperor {{ill|Seimu|en|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, {{ill|Ninotarikahatahi-no-Mikoto|en|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, a seventh-generation descendant, was appointed the first Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atae|en|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the first ruler of {{ill|Shimo Unakami Kuni|en|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|en|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}.
The Kamitsu Unakami lineage shares ties with prominent clans, including the {{ill|Izumo-no-Kami Clan|en|еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ }} and the {{ill|Musashi-no-Kami Clan|en|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}. Notably, the {{ill|Shang-Kazusa Clan|en|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}, mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}, likely descends from the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko.
== Associated Shrines == The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|en|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ}}, dedicated to regional deities, is linked to the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. The shrine enshrines: * {{ill|Ninotarikahatahi-no-Mikoto|en|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, the first Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * Other local deities tied to the {{ill|Anesaki Kofun Group|en|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}.

The {{ill|Kuni no Miyatsuko Registry|en|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}} states that during Emperor {{ill|Seimu|en|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, {{ill|Ninotarikahatahi-no-Mikoto|en|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, a seventh-generation descendant, was appointed the first Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atae|en|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the first ruler of {{ill|Shimo Unakami Kuni|en|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|en|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}.
The Kamitsu Unakami lineage shares ties with prominent clans, including the {{ill|Izumo-no-Kami Clan|en|еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ }} and the {{ill|Musashi-no-Kami Clan|en|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}. Notably, the {{ill|Shang-Kazusa Clan|en|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}, mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}, likely descends from the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko.
== Associated Shrines == The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|en|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ}}, dedicated to regional deities, is linked to the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. The shrine enshrines: * {{ill|Ninotarikahatahi-no-Mikoto|en|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, the first Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * Other local deities tied to the {{ill|Anesaki Kofun Group|en|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}.

[[Category:Kazusa Province]] [[Category:Kuni no Miyatsuko]] [[Category:Chiba Prefecture history]] [[Category:Japanese historical titles]] [[Category:Https://ja.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=]][[Category:Pages_with_redlinks_to_fill]][[Category:pp]][[category:Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:bump]] [[ja:%E4%B8%8A%E6%B5%B7%E4%B8%8A%E5%9B%BD%E9%80%A0]]

{{Infobox historical administrative title | name = Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko (Shang-Kamitsu-Kamiko) | native_name = дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ | period = [[Kofun period|3rd to 6th centuries]] | region = [[Kazusa Province]] ([[Chiba Prefecture]]) | governing_body = [[Kuni no Miyatsuko|Regional chief]] under the [[Yamato court]] }}
'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' ({{ill|Shang-Kamitsu-Kamiko|en|дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }}, lit. "Upper Kamitsu Country Chief"), also known as '''Kamitsu Unakami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko''', was a [[Kuni no Miyatsuko|regional ruler]] of the [[Kazusa Province|Kazusa region]] (modern-day {{ill|Ichihara, Chiba|en|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ}}) in ancient Japan. The title "Kamitsu Unakami" refers to governance over the upper reaches of the [[Unegawa|Unakami River basin]].
== Overview == The territory governed by the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko included areas along the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|en|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin. Archaeological finds from the area suggest early ties to the [[Yamato court]]: * The {{ill|Kamito Kofun Group|en|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} includes [[Makimuku-style tumuli|Makimuku-type tumuli]] dating to the {{ill|3rd century|en|3rd century CE}}, possibly early [[kofun]] or {{ill|Yayoi burial mounds|en|ејҘз”ҹеўідёҳеў“}}. * The {{ill|Inaridai Tomb No. 1|en|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі}} yielded a famous [[iron sword inscription|inscribed iron sword]] bearing the {{ill|King's Reward Inscription|en|зҺӢиіңйҠҳ}}.
The proximity of large-scale tumuli such as the {{ill|Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|en|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} further reinforces the region's prominence as a political center during the Kofun period. An early connection with maritime trade is suggested by a poem in the {{ill|Man'yЕҚshЕ«|en|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}, which describes the Kamitsu Unakami lagoon as a vital point for {{ill|waterborne transport|en|ж°ҙдёҠдәӨйҖҡ}}.
Scholars hypothesize that the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko initially governed a broad swath of central {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|еҚғи‘үзңҢ}}, extending into parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|en|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}. The later establishment of neighboring polities such as {{ill|Musha Kuni|en|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪ}} likely reflects administrative reorganization by the Yamato court.
During the {{ill|5th century|en|5th century CE}}, the Kamitsu Unakami polity is thought to have been subdivided: 1. The emergence of {{ill|Shimo Unakami Kuni|en|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} in the southern basin. 2. Competition from other rising local powers, such as {{ill|Kikuma Kuni|en|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }}, weakened Kamitsu Unakami's influence.
Evidence of this decline is seen in the {{ill|Anesaki Kofun Group|en|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}. The construction of large tumuli, such as the 130-meter {{ill|Anesaki Tenjinyama Kofun|en|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late {{ill|4th century|en|4th century CE}}) and the 103-meter {{ill|Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|en|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (late {{ill|5th century|en|5th century CE}}), shows early prosperity. However, tumuli from later periods diminish in scale, underscoring the polity's waning power.
== Clan Lineage == According to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}, the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko traced its origins to {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|en|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}, a son of {{ill|Amaterasu|en|еӨ©з…§еӨ§зҘһ}}. Specifically: * Founder: {{ill|Takerihatori-no-Mikoto|en|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ}}, a descendant of Ame-no-Hohi.
The {{ill|Kuni no Miyatsuko Registry|en|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}} states that during Emperor {{ill|Seimu|en|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, {{ill|Ninotarikahatahi-no-Mikoto|en|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, a seventh-generation descendant, was appointed the first Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atae|en|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the first ruler of {{ill|Shimo Unakami Kuni|en|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|en|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}.
The Kamitsu Unakami lineage shares ties with prominent clans, including the {{ill|Izumo-no-Kami Clan|en|еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ }} and the {{ill|Musashi-no-Kami Clan|en|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}. Notably, the {{ill|Shang-Kazusa Clan|en|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}, mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}, likely descends from the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko.
== Associated Shrines == The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|en|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ}}, dedicated to regional deities, is linked to the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. The shrine enshrines: * {{ill|Ninotarikahatahi-no-Mikoto|en|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, the first Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * Other local deities tied to the {{ill|Anesaki Kofun Group|en|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}.

The Kamitsu Unakami lineage shares ties with prominent clans, including the {{ill|Izumo-no-Kami Clan|en|еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ }} and the {{ill|Musashi-no-Kami Clan|en|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}. Notably, the {{ill|Shang-Kazusa Clan|en|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}, mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}, likely descends from the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko.
== Associated Shrines == The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|en|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ}}, dedicated to regional deities, is linked to the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. The shrine enshrines: * {{ill|Ninotarikahatahi-no-Mikoto|en|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, the first Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * Other local deities tied to the {{ill|Anesaki Kofun Group|en|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}

[[Category:Kazusa Province]] [[Category:Kuni no Miyatsuko]] [[Category:Chiba Prefecture history]] [[Category:Japanese historical titles]] [[Category:Https://ja.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=]][[Category:Pages_with_redlinks_to_fill]][[Category:pp]][[category:Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:dardar]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[category:original pages]] [[ja:%E4%B8%8A%E6%B5%B7%E4%B8%8A%E5%9B%BD%E9%80%A0]]

{{Infobox historical administrative title | name = Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko (Shang-Kamitsu-Kamiko) | native_name = дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ | period = [[Kofun period|3rd to 6th centuries]] | region = [[Kazusa Province]] ([[Chiba Prefecture]]) | governing_body = [[Kuni no Miyatsuko|Regional chief]] under the [[Yamato court]] }}
'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' ({{ill|Shang-Kamitsu-Kamiko|en|дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }}, lit. "Upper Kamitsu Country Chief"), also known as '''Kamitsu Unakami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko''', was a [[Kuni no Miyatsuko|regional ruler]] of the [[Kazusa Province|Kazusa region]] (modern-day {{ill|Ichihara, Chiba|en|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ}}) in ancient Japan. The title "Kamitsu Unakami" refers to governance over the upper reaches of the [[Unegawa|Unakami River basin]].
== Overview == The territory governed by the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko included areas along the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|en|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin. Archaeological finds from the area suggest early ties to the [[Yamato court]]: * The {{ill|Kamito Kofun Group|en|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} includes [[Makimuku-style tumuli|Makimuku-type tumuli]] dating to the {{ill|3rd century|en|3rd century CE}}, possibly early [[kofun]] or {{ill|Yayoi burial mounds|en|ејҘз”ҹеўідёҳеў“}}. * The {{ill|Inaridai Tomb No. 1|en|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі}} yielded a famous [[iron sword inscription|inscribed iron sword]] bearing the {{ill|King's Reward Inscription|en|зҺӢиіңйҠҳ}}.
The proximity of large-scale tumuli such as the {{ill|Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|en|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} further reinforces the region's prominence as a political center during the Kofun period. An early connection with maritime trade is suggested by a poem in the {{ill|Man'yЕҚshЕ«|en|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}, which describes the Kamitsu Unakami lagoon as a vital point for {{ill|waterborne transport|en|ж°ҙдёҠдәӨйҖҡ}}.
Scholars hypothesize that the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko initially governed a broad swath of central {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|еҚғи‘үзңҢ}}, extending into parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|en|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}. The later establishment of neighboring polities such as {{ill|Musha Kuni|en|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪ}} likely reflects administrative reorganization by the Yamato court.
During the {{ill|5th century|en|5th century CE}}, the Kamitsu Unakami polity is thought to have been subdivided: 1. The emergence of {{ill|Shimo Unakami Kuni|en|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} in the southern basin. 2. Competition from other rising local powers, such as {{ill|Kikuma Kuni|en|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }}, weakened Kamitsu Unakami's influence.
Evidence of this decline is seen in the {{ill|Anesaki Kofun Group|en|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}. The construction of large tumuli, such as the 130-meter {{ill|Anesaki Tenjinyama Kofun|en|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late {{ill|4th century|en|4th century CE}}) and the 103-meter {{ill|Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|en|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (late {{ill|5th century|en|5th century CE}}), shows early prosperity. However, tumuli from later periods diminish in scale, underscoring the polity's waning power.
== Clan Lineage == According to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}, the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko traced its origins to {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|en|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}, a son of {{ill|Amaterasu|en|еӨ©з…§еӨ§зҘһ}}. Specifically: * Founder: {{ill|Takerihatori-no-Mikoto|en|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ}}, a descendant of Ame-no-Hohi.
The {{ill|Kuni no Miyatsuko Registry|en|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}} states that during Emperor {{ill|Seimu|en|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, {{ill|Ninotarikahatahi-no-Mikoto|en|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, a seventh-generation descendant, was appointed the first Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atae|en|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the first ruler of {{ill|Shimo Unakami Kuni|en|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|en|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}.
The Kamitsu Unakami lineage shares ties with prominent clans, including the {{ill|Izumo-no-Kami Clan|en|еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ }} and the {{ill|Musashi-no-Kami Clan|en|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}. Notably, the {{ill|Shang-Kazusa Clan|en|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}, mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}, likely descends from the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko.
== Associated Shrines == The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|en|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ}}, dedicated to regional deities, is linked to the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. The shrine enshrines: * {{ill|Ninotarikahatahi-no-Mikoto|en|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, the first Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * Other local deities tied to the {{ill|Anesaki Kofun Group|en|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Kokushi Daijiten'' ({{ill|Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan|en|еҗүе·қејҳж–ҮйӨЁ}}). * Sakamoto TarЕҚ and Hirano Kunio. ''Nihon Kodai Shizoku Jinmei Jiten.'' Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan. * HЕҚga Toshio. ''Koshi and Izumo no Yoake.'' HЕҚrei Shuppan, 2008.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kuni no Miyatsuko|en|еӣҪйҖ гҒ®дёҖиҰ§}}
== External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/kamitu.html Exploring BЕҚsЕҚ's Ancient Tumuli - Shanghai Kamitsu Region Burial Mounds]

His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atae|en|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the first ruler of {{ill|Shimo Unakami Kuni|en|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|en|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}.
The Kamitsu Unakami lineage shares ties with prominent clans, including the {{ill|Izumo-no-Kami Clan|en|еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ }} and the {{ill|Musashi-no-Kami Clan|en|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}. Notably, the {{ill|Shang-Kazusa Clan|en|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}, mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}, likely descends from the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko.
== Associated Shrines == The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|en|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ}}, dedicated to regional deities, is linked to the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. The shrine enshrines: * {{ill|Ninotarikahatahi-no-Mikoto|en|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, the first Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * Other local deities tied to the {{ill|Anesaki Kofun Group|en|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}.

{{Infobox historical administrative title | name = Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko (Shang-Kamitsu-Kamiko) | native_name = дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ | period = [[Kofun period|3rd to 6th centuries]] | region = [[Kazusa Province]] ([[Chiba Prefecture]]) | governing_body = [[Kuni no Miyatsuko|Regional chief]] under the [[Yamato court]] }}
'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' ({{ill|Shang-Kamitsu-Kamiko|ja|дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }}, lit. "Upper Kamitsu Country Chief"), also known as '''Kamitsu Unakami Kuni-no-Miyatsuko''', was a [[Kuni no Miyatsuko|regional ruler]] of the [[Kazusa Province|Kazusa region]] (modern-day {{ill|Ichihara, Chiba|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ}}) in ancient Japan. The title "Kamitsu Unakami" refers to governance over the upper reaches of the [[Unegawa|Unakami River basin]].
== Overview == The territory governed by the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko included areas along the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin. Archaeological finds from the area suggest early ties to the [[Yamato court]]: * The {{ill|Kamito Kofun Group|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} includes [[Makimuku-style tumuli|Makimuku-type tumuli]] dating to the {{ill|3rd century|ja|3rd century CE}}, possibly early [[kofun]] or {{ill|Yayoi burial mounds|ja|ејҘз”ҹеўідёҳеў“}}. * The {{ill|Inaridai Tomb No. 1|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі}} yielded a famous [[iron sword inscription|inscribed iron sword]] bearing the {{ill|King's Reward Inscription|ja|зҺӢиіңйҠҳ}}.
The proximity of large-scale tumuli such as the {{ill|Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} further reinforces the region's prominence as a political center during the Kofun period. An early connection with maritime trade is suggested by a poem in the {{ill|Man'yЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}, which describes the Kamitsu Unakami lagoon as a vital point for {{ill|waterborne transport|ja|ж°ҙдёҠдәӨйҖҡ}}.
Scholars hypothesize that the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko initially governed a broad swath of central {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ}}, extending into parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}. The later establishment of neighboring polities such as {{ill|Musha Kuni|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪ}} likely reflects administrative reorganization by the Yamato court.
During the {{ill|5th century|ja|5th century CE}}, the Kamitsu Unakami polity is thought to have been subdivided: 1. The emergence of {{ill|Shimo Unakami Kuni|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} in the southern basin. 2. Competition from other rising local powers, such as {{ill|Kikuma Kuni|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }}, weakened Kamitsu Unakami's influence.
Evidence of this decline is seen in the {{ill|Anesaki Kofun Group|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}. The construction of large tumuli, such as the 130-meter {{ill|Anesaki Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late {{ill|4th century|ja|4th century CE}}) and the 103-meter {{ill|Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (late {{ill|5th century|ja|5th century CE}}), shows early prosperity. However, tumuli from later periods diminish in scale, underscoring the polity's waning power.
== Clan Lineage == According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}, the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko traced its origins to {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}, a son of {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|еӨ©з…§еӨ§зҘһ}}. Specifically: * Founder: {{ill|Takerihatori-no-Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ}}, a descendant of Ame-no-Hohi.
The {{ill|Kuni no Miyatsuko Registry|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}} states that during Emperor {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, {{ill|Ninotarikahatahi-no-Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, a seventh-generation descendant, was appointed the first Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atae|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the first ruler of {{ill|Shimo Unakami Kuni|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}.
The Kamitsu Unakami lineage shares ties with prominent clans, including the {{ill|Izumo-no-Kami Clan|ja|еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ }} and the {{ill|Musashi-no-Kami Clan|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}. Notably, the {{ill|Shang-Kazusa Clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}, mentioned in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}, likely descends from the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko.
== Associated Shrines == The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ}}, dedicated to regional deities, is linked to the Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. The shrine enshrines: * {{ill|Ninotarikahatahi-no-Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, the first Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * Other local deities tied to the {{ill|Anesaki Kofun Group|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * ''Kokushi Daijiten'' ({{ill|Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan|ja|еҗүе·қејҳж–ҮйӨЁ}}). * Sakamoto TarЕҚ and Hirano Kunio. ''Nihon Kodai Shizoku Jinmei Jiten.'' Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan. * HЕҚga Toshio. ''Koshi and Izumo no Yoake.'' HЕҚrei Shuppan, 2008.

[[Category:Kazusa Province]] [[Category:Kuni no Miyatsuko]] [[Category:Chiba Prefecture history]] [[Category:Japanese historical titles]] [[Category:Https://ja.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=]][[Category:Pages_with_redlinks_to_fill]][[Category:pp]][[category:Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:dardar]] [[Category:гғ•гӮЎгӮӨгғ«]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[ja:%E4%B8%8A%E6%B5%B7%E4%B8%8A%E5%9B%BD%E9%80%A0]]

'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ , also known as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ) governed the {{ill|KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|ja|жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)}} in {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ}}, which corresponds to part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ}}. It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inaritai No.1 Tumulus|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}. In addition, the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ }} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Tumulus Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Tumulus|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Tumulus (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ }} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.
The {{ill|Ane Saki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Tumulus Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{ill|Historical Dictionary of National History|ja|еңӢеҸІеӨ§иҫӯе…ё}}, Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan. * Sakamoto TarЕҚ & Hirano Kunio, ''Dictionary of Japanese Clan Names,'' Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan. * ''Encyclopedia of Japanese History,'' Yamakawa Publishing. * ''Great Dictionary of Shinto,'' Rinsen Shoten. * HЕҚga Toshio, ''The Dawn of Koshin and Izumo,'' HЕҚrei Publishing, 2008.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|List of KokuzЕҚ|ja|еӣҪйҖ гҒ®дёҖиҰ§}}
== External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/kamitu.html Walking through the Ancient Tumuli of Kamitsu Unakami Kuni]

His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}. The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.
The {{ill|Ane Saki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Tumulus Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.

== Bibliography == * {{ill|Historical Dictionary of National History|ja|еңӢеҸІеӨ§иҫӯе…ё}}, Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan. * Sakamoto TarЕҚ & Hirano Kunio, ''Dictionary of Japanese Clan Names,'' Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan. * ''Encyclopedia of Japanese History,'' Yamakawa Publishing. * ''Great Dictionary of Shinto,'' Rinsen Shoten. * HЕҚga Toshio, ''The Dawn of Koshin and Izumo,'' HЕҚrei Publishing, 2008.

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[Kamiagami no Kuni Miyatsuko]] to [[Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]
revision.text:
'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ , also known as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ) governed the {{ill|KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|ja|жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)}} in {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ}}, which corresponds to part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ}}. It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inaritai No.1 Tumulus|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}. In addition, the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ }} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Tumulus Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Tumulus|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Tumulus (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ }} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.
The {{ill|Ane Saki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Tumulus Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{ill|Historical Dictionary of National History|ja|еңӢеҸІеӨ§иҫӯе…ё}}, Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan. * Sakamoto TarЕҚ & Hirano Kunio, ''Dictionary of Japanese Clan Names,'' Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan. * ''Encyclopedia of Japanese History,'' Yamakawa Publishing. * ''Great Dictionary of Shinto,'' Rinsen Shoten. * HЕҚga Toshio, ''The Dawn of Koshin and Izumo,'' HЕҚrei Publishing, 2008.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|List of KokuzЕҚ|ja|еӣҪйҖ гҒ®дёҖиҰ§}}
== External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/kamitu.html Walking through the Ancient Tumuli of Kamitsu Unakami Kuni]

The {{ill|Ane Saki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Tumulus Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.

[[Category:Kazusa Province]] [[Category:KokuzЕҚ]] [[Category:History of Chiba Prefecture]] [[category:most recent 500]][[ja:дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ ]]

'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ , also known as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ) governed the {{ill|KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|ja|жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)}} in {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ}}, which corresponds to part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ}}. It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inaritai No.1 Tumulus|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}. In addition, the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ }} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Tumulus Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Tumulus|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Tumulus (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ }} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.
The {{ill|Ane Saki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Tumulus Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.

[[Category:Kazusa Province]] [[Category:KokuzЕҚ]] [[Category:History of Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ ]]

'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ , also known as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ) governed the {{ill|KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|ja|жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)}} in {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ}}, which corresponds to part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ}}. It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inaritai No.1 Tumulus|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}. In addition, the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ }} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Tumulus Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Tumulus|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Tumulus (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ }} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.
The {{ill|Ane Saki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Tumulus Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.

'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ , also known as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ) governed the {{ill|KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|ja|жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)}} in {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ}}, which corresponds to part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ}}. It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inari Daichi Kofun|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі|Inari Daichi Kofun=Inari Daichi Kofun|lt=Inaritai No.1 Tumulus}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}. In addition, the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ }} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Tumulus Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Tumulus|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Tumulus (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ }} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Tumulus Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{ill|Historical Dictionary of National History|ja|еңӢеҸІеӨ§иҫӯе…ё}}, Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan. * Sakamoto TarЕҚ & Hirano Kunio, ''Dictionary of Japanese Clan Names,'' Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan. * ''Encyclopedia of Japanese History,'' Yamakawa Publishing. * ''Great Dictionary of Shinto,'' Rinsen Shoten. * HЕҚga Toshio, ''The Dawn of Koshin and Izumo,'' HЕҚrei Publishing, 2008.

[[Category:oldest 100 pages as of Feb 11]][[Category:Kazusa Province]] [[Category:oldest 100 pages as of Feb 11]][[Category:KokuzЕҚ]] [[Category:oldest 100 pages as of Feb 11]][[Category:History of Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:oldest 100 pages as of Feb 11]][[Category:kk]][[Category:oldest 100 pages as of Feb 11]][[Category:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[Category:oldest 100 pages as of Feb 11]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:oldest 100 pages as of Feb 11]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:oldest 100 pages as of Feb 11]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ ]]

'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ , also known as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ) governed the {{ill|KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|ja|жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)}} in {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ}}, which corresponds to part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ}}. It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inari Daichi Kofun|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі|Inari Daichi Kofun=Inari Daichi Kofun|lt=Inaritai No.1 Tumulus}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}. In addition, the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ }} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Tumulus Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Tumulus|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Tumulus (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ }} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Tumulus Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.

The Anegasaki Tumulus Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.

[[Category:oldest 100 pages as of Feb 11]][[Category:Kazusa Province]] [[Category:oldest 100 pages as of Feb 11]][[Category:KokuzЕҚ]] [[Category:oldest 100 pages as of Feb 11]][[Category:History of Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:oldest 100 pages as of Feb 11]][[Category:kk]][[Category:oldest 100 pages as of Feb 11]][[Category:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[Category:oldest 100 pages as of Feb 11]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:oldest 100 pages as of Feb 11]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:oldest 100 pages as of Feb 11]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[ja:дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ ]]

'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ , also known as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ) governed the {{ill|KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|ja|жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)}} in {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ}}, which corresponds to part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ}}. It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inari Daichi Kofun|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі|Inari Daichi Kofun=Inari Daichi Kofun|lt=Inaritai No.1 Tumulus}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}. In addition, the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ }} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Tumulus Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Tumulus|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Tumulus (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ }} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Tumulus Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
