'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ , also known as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ) governed the {{ill|KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|ja|жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)}} in {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ}}, which corresponds to part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ}}. It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inaridai No.1 Kofun|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі|Inaridai No.1 Kofun=Inaridai No.1 Kofun|lt=Inaritai No.1 Tumulus}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}. In addition, the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ }} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Tumulus Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Tumulus|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Tumulus (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ }} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Tumulus Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.

'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ , also known as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ) governed the {{ill|KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|ja|жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)}} in {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ}}, which corresponds to part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ}}. It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inaridai No.1 Kofun|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі|Inaridai No.1 Kofun=Inaridai No.1 Kofun|lt=Inaritai No.1 Tumulus}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}. In addition, the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ }} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Tumulus Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Tumulus|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Tumulus (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ }} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Tumulus Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.

'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ , also known as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ) governed the {{ill|KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|ja|жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)}} in {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ}}, which corresponds to part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ}}. It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inaridai No.1 Kofun|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі|Inaridai No.1 Kofun=Inaridai No.1 Kofun|lt=Inaritai No.1 Tumulus}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}. In addition, the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ }} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Tumulus Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Tumulus|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Tumulus (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ }} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Tumulus Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.

His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ }} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later aqqed {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Tumulus Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.

[[Category:oldest 100 pages as of Feb 11]][[Category:Kazusa Province]] [[Category:oldest 100 pages as of Feb 11]][[Category:KokuzЕҚ]] [[Category:oldest 100 pages as of Feb 11]][[Category:History of Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:oldest 100 pages as of Feb 11]] [[Category:oldest 100 pages as of Feb 11]][[Category:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[Category:oldest 100 pages as of Feb 11]] [[Category:oldest 100 pages as of Feb 11]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:oldest 100 pages as of Feb 11]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko]][[Category:qq]] <!--Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko--> [[Category:qq]][[en:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[sn:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[sn:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[sn:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[sn:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[sn:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[sn:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]][[ja:дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ ]] [[sn:hanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] [[Category:New]]

'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ , also known as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ) governed the {{ill|KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|ja|жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)}} in {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ}}, which corresponds to part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ}}. It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inaridai No.1 Kofun|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі|Inaridai No.1 Kofun=Inaridai No.1 Kofun|lt=Inaritai No.1 Tumulus}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}. In aqqition, the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ }} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Tumulus Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Tumulus|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Tumulus (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ }} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later aqqed {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Tumulus Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.

Kuni no Miyatsuko]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko]][[Category:qq]]

'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ , also known as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ) governed the {{ill|KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|ja|жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)}} in {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ}}, which corresponds to part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ}}. It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inaridai No.1 Kofun|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі|Inaridai No.1 Kofun=Inaridai No.1 Kofun|lt=Inaritai No.1 Tumulus}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}. In aqqition, the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ }} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Tumulus Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Tumulus|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Tumulus (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ }} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later aqqed {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Tumulus Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.

By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Kofun (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ }} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later aqqed {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Kofun Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.

'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ , also known as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ) governed the {{ill|KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|ja|жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)}} in {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ}}, which corresponds to part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ}}. It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ}}. Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later aqqed {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Kofun Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.

[[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ , also known as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ) governed the {{ill|KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|ja|жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)}} in {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ}}, which corresponds to part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ}}. It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inaridai No.1 Kofun|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі|Inaridai No.1 Kofun=Inaridai No.1 Kofun|lt=Inaritai No.1 Kofun}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}. In aqqition, the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ }} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Kofun (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|Izumo Province йҖ }} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later aqqed {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Kofun Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.

'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ , also known as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ) governed the {{ill|KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|ja|жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)}} in {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ}}, which corresponds to part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ}}. It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inaridai No.1 Kofun|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі|Inaridai No.1 Kofun=Inaridai No.1 Kofun|lt=Inaritai No.1 Kofun}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}. In a11ition, the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ }} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Kofun (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|Izumo Province йҖ }} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ|1=Ame-no-Koyane}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later a11ed {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Kofun Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.

The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ }} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Kofun (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|Izumo Province йҖ }} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ|1=Ame-no-Koyane}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later a11ed {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Kofun Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{ill|Historical Dictionary of National History|ja|еңӢеҸІеӨ§иҫӯе…ё}}, Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan. * Sakamoto TarЕҚ & Hirano Kunio, ''Dictionary of Japanese Clan Names,'' Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan. * ''Encyclopedia of Japanese History,'' Yamakawa Publishing. * ''Great Dictionary of Shinto,'' Rinsen Shoten. * HЕҚga Toshio, ''The Dawn of Koshin and Izumo,'' HЕҚrei Publishing, 2008.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of KokuzЕҚ|ja|еӣҪйҖ гҒ®дёҖиҰ§}}

'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ , also known as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ) governed the {{ill|KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|ja|жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)}} in {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ}}, which corresponds to part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ}}. It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inaridai No.1 Kofun|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі|Inaridai No.1 Kofun=Inaridai No.1 Kofun|lt=Inaritai No.1 Kofun}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}. In addition[[Category:addition]], the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ }} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Kofun (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|Izumo Province йҖ }} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.

His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Kofun Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.

The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|Izumo Province йҖ }} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ|1=Ame-no-Koyane}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Kofun Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.

'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ , also known as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ) governed the {{ill|KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|ja|жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)}} in {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ}}, which corresponds to part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ}}. It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inaridai No.1 Kofun|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі|Inaridai No.1 Kofun=Inaridai No.1 Kofun|lt=Inaritai No.1 Kofun}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}. In addition[[Category:addition]], the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ }} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Kofun (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|Izumo Province йҖ }} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ|1=Ame-no-Koyane}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Kofun Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.

'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ , also known as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ) governed the {{ill|KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|ja|жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)}} in {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ}}, which corresponds to part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ}}. It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inaridai No.1 Kofun|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі|Inaridai No.1 Kofun=Inaridai No.1 Kofun|lt=Inaritai No.1 Kofun}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}. In addition[[Category:addition]], the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ }} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Kofun (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|Izumo Province йҖ }} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ|1=Ame-no-Koyane}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Kofun Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.

It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inaridai No.1 Kofun|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі|Inaridai No.1 Kofun=Inaridai No.1 Kofun|lt=Inaritai No.1 Kofun}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}. In addition[[Category:addition]], the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ }} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Kofun (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|Izumo Province йҖ }} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°|1=Sukune}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ|1=Ame-no-Koyane}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Kofun Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.

=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ|1=Takehi-Nateru|zh|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ|fr}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|Izumo Province йҖ }} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°|1=Sukune}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ|1=Ame-no-Koyane}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Kofun Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{ill|Historical Dictionary of National History|ja|еңӢеҸІеӨ§иҫӯе…ё}}, Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan. * Sakamoto TarЕҚ & Hirano Kunio, ''Dictionary of Japanese Clan Names,'' Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan. * ''Encyclopedia of Japanese History,'' Yamakawa Publishing. * ''Great Dictionary of Shinto,'' Rinsen Shoten. * HЕҚga Toshio, ''The Dawn of Koshin and Izumo,'' HЕҚrei Publishing, 2008.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of KokuzЕҚ|ja|еӣҪйҖ гҒ®дёҖиҰ§}}

revision.text:
'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ , also known as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ) governed the {{ill|KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|ja|жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)}} in {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ}}, which corresponds to part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ}}. It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inaridai No.1 Kofun|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі|Inaridai No.1 Kofun=Inaridai No.1 Kofun|lt=Inaritai No.1 Kofun}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}. In addition[[Category:addition]], the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ }} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Kofun (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ|1=Takehi-Nateru|zh|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ|fr}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|Izumo Province йҖ }} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°|1=Sukune}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ|1=Ame-no-Koyane}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Kofun Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{ill|Historical Dictionary of National History|ja|еңӢеҸІеӨ§иҫӯе…ё}}, Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan. * Sakamoto TarЕҚ & Hirano Kunio, ''Dictionary of Japanese Clan Names,'' Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan. * ''Encyclopedia of Japanese History,'' Yamakawa Publishing. * ''Great Dictionary of Shinto,'' Rinsen Shoten. * HЕҚga Toshio, ''The Dawn of Koshin and Izumo,'' HЕҚrei Publishing, 2008.== See Also == * {{ill|List of KokuzЕҚ|ja|еӣҪйҖ гҒ®дёҖиҰ§}}

Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.

His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Kofun Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "== See Also ==" to " == See Also! =="
revision.text:
'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ , also known as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ) governed the {{ill|KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|ja|жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)}} in {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ}}, which corresponds to part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ}}. It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inaridai No.1 Kofun|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі|Inaridai No.1 Kofun=Inaridai No.1 Kofun|lt=Inaritai No.1 Kofun}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}. In addition[[Category:addition]], the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ }} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Kofun (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ|1=Takehi-Nateru|zh|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ|fr}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|Izumo Province йҖ }} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°|1=Sukune}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ|1=Ame-no-Koyane}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Kofun Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{ill|Historical Dictionary of National History|ja|еңӢеҸІеӨ§иҫӯе…ё}}, Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan. * Sakamoto TarЕҚ & Hirano Kunio, ''Dictionary of Japanese Clan Names,'' Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan. * ''Encyclopedia of Japanese History,'' Yamakawa Publishing. * ''Great Dictionary of Shinto,'' Rinsen Shoten. * HЕҚga Toshio, ''The Dawn of Koshin and Izumo,'' HЕҚrei Publishing, 2008. == See Also! == * {{ill|List of KokuzЕҚ|ja|еӣҪйҖ гҒ®дёҖиҰ§}}
== External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/kamitu.html Walking through the Ancient Tumuli of Kamitsu Unakami Kuni]
