revision.text:
'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ , also known as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ) governed the {{ill|KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|ja|жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)}} in {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ}}, which corresponds to part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ}}. It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inaridai No.1 Kofun|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі|Inaridai No.1 Kofun=Inaridai No.1 Kofun|lt=Inaritai No.1 Kofun}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}. In addition[[Category:addition]], the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ }} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Kofun (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ|1=Takehi-Nateru|zh|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ|fr}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|Izumo Province йҖ }} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°|1=Sukune}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ|1=Ame-no-Koyane}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Kofun Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{ill|Historical Dictionary of National History|ja|еңӢеҸІеӨ§иҫӯе…ё}}, Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan. * Sakamoto TarЕҚ & Hirano Kunio, ''Dictionary of Japanese Clan Names,'' Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan. * ''Encyclopedia of Japanese History,'' Yamakawa Publishing. * ''Great Dictionary of Shinto,'' Rinsen Shoten. * HЕҚga Toshio, ''The Dawn of Koshin and Izumo,'' HЕҚrei Publishing, 2008. == See Also == * {{ill|List of KokuzЕҚ|ja|еӣҪйҖ гҒ®дёҖиҰ§}}
== External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/kamitu.html Walking through the Ancient Tumuli of Kamitsu Unakami Kuni]

It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inaridai No.1 Kofun|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі|Inaridai No.1 Kofun=Inaridai No.1 Kofun|lt=Inaritai No.1 Kofun}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}. In addition, the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ }} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ }} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ }} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Kofun (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ|1=Takehi-Nateru|zh|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ|fr}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|Izumo Province йҖ }} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ }}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°|1=Sukune}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ|1=Ame-no-Koyane}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Kofun Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{ill|Historical Dictionary of National History|ja|еңӢеҸІеӨ§иҫӯе…ё}}, Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan. * Sakamoto TarЕҚ & Hirano Kunio, ''Dictionary of Japanese Clan Names,'' Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan. * ''Encyclopedia of Japanese History,'' Yamakawa Publishing. * ''Great Dictionary of Shinto,'' Rinsen Shoten. * HЕҚga Toshio, ''The Dawn of Koshin and Izumo,'' HЕҚrei Publishing, 2008. == See Also == * {{ill|List of KokuzЕҚ|ja|еӣҪйҖ гҒ®дёҖиҰ§}}
== External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/kamitu.html Walking through the Ancient Tumuli of Kamitsu Unakami Kuni]

revision.text:
'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ , also known as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ) governed the {{ill|KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|ja|жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)|12=draft}} in {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ|12=draft}}, which corresponds to part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ|12=draft}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ|12=draft}}. It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ|12=draft}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ|12=draft}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј|12=draft}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inaridai No.1 Kofun|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі|Inaridai No.1 Kofun=Inaridai No.1 Kofun|lt=Inaritai No.1 Kofun|12=draft}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ|12=draft}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|12=draft}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©|12=draft}}. In addition, the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ|12=draft}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ |12=draft}} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ |12=draft}} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ|12=draft}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ|12=draft}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ |12=draft}} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ|12=draft}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ|12=draft}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі|12=draft}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Kofun (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ|12=draft}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ|12=draft}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ|1=Takehi-Nateru|zh|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ|fr|12=draft}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ|12=draft}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ|12=draft}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ|12=draft}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ|12=draft}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ|12=draft}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ|12=draft}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|Izumo Province йҖ |12=draft}} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ |12=draft}}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ|12=draft}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ|12=draft}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ|12=draft}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°|1=Sukune|12=draft}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ|12=draft}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ|12=draft}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki|12=draft}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ|1=Ame-no-Koyane|12=draft}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ|12=draft}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Kofun Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ|12=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{ill|Historical Dictionary of National History|ja|еңӢеҸІеӨ§иҫӯе…ё|12=draft}}, Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan. * Sakamoto TarЕҚ & Hirano Kunio, ''Dictionary of Japanese Clan Names,'' Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan. * ''Encyclopedia of Japanese History,'' Yamakawa Publishing. * ''Great Dictionary of Shinto,'' Rinsen Shoten. * HЕҚga Toshio, ''The Dawn of Koshin and Izumo,'' HЕҚrei Publishing, 2008.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of KokuzЕҚ|ja|еӣҪйҖ гҒ®дёҖиҰ§|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/kamitu.html Walking through the Ancient Tumuli of Kamitsu Unakami Kuni]

The {{ill|Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ|12=draft|12=draft}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі|12=draft|12=draft}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Kofun (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ|12=draft|12=draft}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ|12=draft|12=draft}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ|1=Takehi-Nateru|zh|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ|fr|12=draft|12=draft}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ|12=draft|12=draft}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ|12=draft|12=draft}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ|12=draft|12=draft}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ|12=draft|12=draft}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ|12=draft|12=draft}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ|12=draft|12=draft}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|Izumo Province йҖ |12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ |12=draft|12=draft}}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ|12=draft|12=draft}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ|12=draft|12=draft}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ|12=draft|12=draft}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°|1=Sukune|12=draft|12=draft}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ|12=draft|12=draft}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ|12=draft|12=draft}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki|12=draft|12=draft}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ|1=Ame-no-Koyane|12=draft|12=draft}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ|12=draft|12=draft}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Kofun Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ|12=draft|12=draft}}.

{{draft categories| [[Category:addition]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Kazusa Province]] [[Category:KokuzЕҚ]] [[Category:History of Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]] }} [[sn:Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko]] [[en:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] [[sn:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] [[ja:дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ ]] [[sn:hanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko}}

'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ , also known as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ) governed the {{ill|KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|ja|жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ|12=draft|12=draft}}, which corresponds to part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ|12=draft|12=draft}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ|12=draft|12=draft}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft|12=draft}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј|12=draft|12=draft}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inaridai No.1 Kofun|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі|Inaridai No.1 Kofun=Inaridai No.1 Kofun|lt=Inaritai No.1 Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©|12=draft|12=draft}}. In addition, the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ|12=draft|12=draft}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ |12=draft|12=draft}} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ |12=draft|12=draft}} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ|12=draft|12=draft}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ |12=draft|12=draft}} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ|12=draft|12=draft}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ|12=draft|12=draft}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі|12=draft|12=draft}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Kofun (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.

The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ|12=draft|12=draft}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ|12=draft|12=draft}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ|12=draft|12=draft}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ|12=draft|12=draft}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ|12=draft|12=draft}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ|12=draft|12=draft}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|Izumo Province йҖ |12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ |12=draft|12=draft}}.

Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ|12=draft|12=draft}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ|12=draft|12=draft}}.

revision.text:
'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ , also known as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ) governed the {{ill|KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|ja|жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ|12=draft|12=draft}}, which corresponds to part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ|12=draft|12=draft}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ|12=draft|12=draft}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft|12=draft}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј|12=draft|12=draft}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inaridai No.1 Kofun|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі|Inaridai No.1 Kofun=Inaridai No.1 Kofun|lt=Inaritai No.1 Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©|12=draft|12=draft}}. In addition, the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ|12=draft|12=draft}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ |12=draft|12=draft}} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ |12=draft|12=draft}} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ|12=draft|12=draft}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ |12=draft|12=draft}} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ|12=draft|12=draft}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ|12=draft|12=draft}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі|12=draft|12=draft}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Kofun (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ|12=draft|12=draft}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ|12=draft|12=draft}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ|1=Takehi-Nateru|zh|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ|fr|12=draft|12=draft}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ|12=draft|12=draft}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ|12=draft|12=draft}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ|12=draft|12=draft}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ|12=draft|12=draft}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ|12=draft|12=draft}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ|12=draft|12=draft}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|Izumo Province йҖ |12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ |12=draft|12=draft}}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ|12=draft|12=draft}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ|12=draft|12=draft}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ|12=draft|12=draft}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°|1=Sukune|12=draft|12=draft}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ|12=draft|12=draft}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ|12=draft|12=draft}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki|12=draft|12=draft}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ|1=Ame-no-Koyane|12=draft|12=draft}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ|12=draft|12=draft}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Kofun Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ|12=draft|12=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{ill|Historical Dictionary of National History|ja|еңӢеҸІеӨ§иҫӯе…ё|12=draft|12=draft}}, Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan. * Sakamoto TarЕҚ & Hirano Kunio, ''Dictionary of Japanese Clan Names,'' Yoshikawa KЕҚbunkan. * ''Encyclopedia of Japanese History,'' Yamakawa Publishing. * ''Great Dictionary of Shinto,'' Rinsen Shoten. * HЕҚga Toshio, ''The Dawn of Koshin and Izumo,'' HЕҚrei Publishing, 2008.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of KokuzЕҚ|ja|еӣҪйҖ гҒ®дёҖиҰ§|12=draft|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/kamitu.html Walking through the Ancient Tumuli of Kamitsu Unakami Kuni]

Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ|12=draft|12=draft}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ|12=draft|12=draft}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ|1=Takehi-Nateru|zh|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ|fr|12=draft|12=draft}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ|12=draft|12=draft}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ|12=draft|12=draft}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ|12=draft|12=draft}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ|12=draft|12=draft}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ|12=draft|12=draft}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ|12=draft|12=draft}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|Izumo Province йҖ |12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ |12=draft|12=draft}}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ|12=draft|12=draft}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ|12=draft|12=draft}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ|12=draft|12=draft}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°|1=Sukune|12=draft|12=draft}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ|12=draft|12=draft}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ|12=draft|12=draft}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki|12=draft|12=draft}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ|1=Ame-no-Koyane|12=draft|12=draft}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ|12=draft|12=draft}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Kofun Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ|12=draft|12=draft}}.

{{draft categories| [[Category:Kazusa Province]] [[Category:KokuzЕҚ]] [[Category:History of Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko]] [[en:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] [[sn:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] [[ja:дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ ]] [[sn:hanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko}}

'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ , also known as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ) governed the {{ill|KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|ja|жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ|12=draft|12=draft}}, which corresponds to part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ|12=draft|12=draft}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ|12=draft|12=draft}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft|12=draft}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј|12=draft|12=draft}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inaridai No.1 Kofun|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі|Inaridai No.1 Kofun=Inaridai No.1 Kofun|lt=Inaritai No.1 Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©|12=draft|12=draft}}. In addition, the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ|12=draft|12=draft}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ |12=draft|12=draft}} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ |12=draft|12=draft}} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ|12=draft|12=draft}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ |12=draft|12=draft}} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ|12=draft|12=draft}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ|12=draft|12=draft}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі|12=draft|12=draft}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Kofun (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.

The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ|12=draft|12=draft}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ|12=draft|12=draft}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ|12=draft|12=draft}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ|12=draft|12=draft}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ|12=draft|12=draft}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ|12=draft|12=draft}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|Izumo Province йҖ |12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ |12=draft|12=draft}}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ|12=draft|12=draft}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ|12=draft|12=draft}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ|12=draft|12=draft}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°|1=Sukune|12=draft|12=draft}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ|12=draft|12=draft}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ|12=draft|12=draft}}.

'''дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ '''пјҲгҒӢгҒҝгҒӨгҒҶгҒӘгҒӢгҒҝгҒ®гҒҸгҒ«гҒҝгӮ„гҒӨгҒ“гғ»гҒӢгҒҝгҒӨгҒҶгҒӘгҒӢгҒҝгҒ“гҒҸгҒһгҒҶпјүгҒҜгҖҒ[[дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ]][[жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)|жө·дёҠйғЎ]]пјҲзҸҫеңЁгҒ®[[еҚғи‘үзңҢ]][[еёӮеҺҹеёӮ]]гҒ®дёҖйғЁпјүгӮ’ж”Ҝй…ҚгҒ—гҒҹ[[еӣҪйҖ ]]гҖӮдёҠиҸҹдёҠеӣҪйҖ гҒЁгӮӮгҖӮ
== жҰӮиҰҒ == й ҳеҹҹгҒ®[[йӨҠиҖҒе·қ]]жөҒеҹҹгҒ«гҒҜгҖҒ[[3дё–зҙҖ]]гҒ®[[еҮәзҸҫжңҹеҸӨеўі]]пјҲ[[зә’еҗ‘еһӢеүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі]]пјүгҒ§гҒӮгӮӢ[[зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ]]<ref>гҒ“гӮҢгӮ’еҸӨеўігҒЁгҒҷгӮӢгҒЁ[[еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј]]гҒ®е§ӢгҒҫгӮҠгҒҢеӨ§е№…гҒ«гҒ•гҒӢгҒ®гҒјгӮӢгҒҹгӮҒгҖҒеҸӨеўігҒ§гҒҜгҒӘгҒҸ[[ејҘз”ҹеўідёҳеў“]]гҒЁгҒҷгӮӢиӘ¬гӮӮгҒӮгӮӢгҖӮ</ref>гӮ„гҖҒ[[йү„еүЈгғ»йү„еҲҖйҠҳж–Ү|зҺӢиіңйҠҳ]]йү„еүЈгӮ’еҮәеңҹгҒ—гҒҹ[[зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі]]<ref>гҒӘгҒҠгҖҒгҒ“гӮҢгӮүйӨҠиҖҒе·қеҢ—еІёгҒ®еҸӨеўізҫӨгҒҜйҰ–й•·гҒ®еўіеў“гҒ§гҒҜгҒӘгҒҸе°ҸеӢўеҠӣгҒ®гӮӮгҒ®гҒ§гҖҒгҒ“гҒ®гҒҹгӮҒзҺӢиіңйҠҳйү„еүЈгҒ®гҖҢзҺӢгҖҚгӮ’гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©гҒ®[[жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ|еӨ§зҺӢ]]гҒЁгҒҷгӮӢд»–гҖҒдёҠжө·дёҠгҒ®йҰ–й•·гҒ§гҒӮгӮӢеҜҫеІёгҒ®е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўігҒ®иў«и‘¬иҖ…гҒЁгҒҝгӮӢиӘ¬гӮӮгҒӮгӮӢгҖӮ</ref>гҒҢгҒӮгӮҠгҖҒеҸӨгҒҸгҒӢгӮү[[гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©]]гҒЁз·ҠеҜҶгҒӘгҒӨгҒӘгҒҢгӮҠгҒҢгҒӮгҒЈгҒҹгҒ“гҒЁгҒҢжғіе®ҡгҒ•гӮҢгӮӢгҖӮгҖҺ[[дёҮи‘үйӣҶ]]гҖҸе·»з¬¬еҚҒеӣӣ[[жқұжӯҢ (е’ҢжӯҢ)|жқұжӯҢ]]гҒ®зӯҶй ӯгҒ«гҖҢеӨҸйә»еј•гҒҸжө·дёҠжҪҹгҒ®жІ–гҒӨжёҡгҒ«иҲ№гҒҜгҒЁгҒ©гӮҒгӮҖе°ҸеӨңгҒөгҒ‘гҒ«гҒ‘гӮҠгҖҚгҒЁгҒӮгӮҠгҖҒ[[ж°ҙдёҠдәӨйҖҡ]]гҒ®иҰҒиЎқгҒ§гҒӮгҒЈгҒҹгҒЁгӮӮгҒ•гӮҢгӮӢгҖӮ
гҒҫгҒҹгҖҒеҪ“еӣҪйҖ гҒ®й ҳеҹҹгҒЁ[[дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ ]]гҒ®й ҳеҹҹгҒ®й–“гҒ«[[жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ ]]гҒ®й ҳеҹҹгҒҢгҒҜгҒ„гҒЈгҒҰгҒ„гӮӢгҒ“гҒЁгҒӢгӮүгҖҒжҲҝз·ҸгҒ®еӣҪйҖ еҲ¶гҒ®еұ•й–ӢгҒ«гҒҜе°‘гҒӘгҒҸгҒЁгӮӮ2ж®өйҡҺгҒҢгҒӮгҒЈгҒҹгҒЁгҒ•гӮҢгҖҒ[[5дё–зҙҖ]]гҒҫгҒ§гҒҜдёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪгӮӮдҪөгҒӣгҖҒеҚғи‘үзңҢдёӯйғЁгҒӢгӮү[[иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ]]гҖҒ[[еҹјзҺүзңҢ]]гҖҒ[[жқұдә¬йғҪ]]гҒ«гҒӢгҒ‘гҒҰгҒ®дёҖеёҜгӮ’ж”Ҝй…ҚгҒ—гҒҹеӨ§еӢўеҠӣгҒ§гҒӮгҒЈгҒҹгҒЁгҒҷгӮӢиӘ¬гӮӮгҒӮгӮӢгҖӮ
гҒқгҒ®еҫҢ[[6дё–зҙҖ]]гҒ«дёӯеӨ®гҒӢгӮүйҖІеҮәгҒ—гҒҹеӢўеҠӣгҒ®е»әгҒҰгҒҹжӯҰзӨҫеӣҪгҒ«гӮҲгҒЈгҒҰдёҠгғ»дёӢгҒ«еҲҶеүІгҒ•гӮҢгҖҒгҒ•гӮүгҒ«[[жқ‘з”°е·қ]]жөҒеҹҹгҒ®[[иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ ]]гҒ®еҸ°й ӯгҒ«гӮҲгӮҠиЎ°йҖҖгӮ’дҪҷе„ҖгҒӘгҒҸгҒ•гӮҢгҒҹгҖӮйӨҠиҖҒе·қдёӢжөҒеҚ—еІёгҒ®[[е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ]]гҒҜеҪ“еӣҪйҖ дёҖж—ҸгҒ®еўіеў“гҒЁгҒ•гӮҢгҖҒ[[4дё–зҙҖ]]еҫҢеҚҠгҒ®е…Ёй•·130гғЎгғјгғҲгғ«гҒ®[[е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі]]гҖҒ5дё–зҙҖеүҚеҚҠгҒ®е…Ёй•·86гғЎгғјгғҲгғ«гҒ®йҮҲиҝҰеұұеҸӨеўігҒ«з¶ҡгҒҚгҖҒ5дё–зҙҖеҫҢеҚҠгҒ«гҒҜе…Ёй•·103гғЎгғјгғҲгғ«гҒ®[[е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі]]гҒҢйҖ е–¶гҒ•гӮҢгҒҰгҒ„гӮӢгҒҢгҖҒ6дё–зҙҖд»ҘйҷҚгҒЁгҒ•гӮҢгӮӢеҸӨеўігҒҢеҫҗгҖ…гҒ«зё®е°ҸгҒ—гҒҰгҒ„гӮӢгҒ“гҒЁгҒӢгӮүгӮӮиЎ°йҖҖгҒҢиЈҸд»ҳгҒ‘гӮүгӮҢгӮӢгҖӮ
=== ж°Ҹж—Ҹ === гҖҺ[[еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ]]гҖҸгҒ§гҒҜ[[гӮўгғЎгғҺгғӣгғ’|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ]]гҒ®еӯҗгҒ®[[е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ]]<ref>е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘ҪгҒҜгҖҒжӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫгғ»еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫгғ»еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘ҪгҒӘгҒ©гҒЁгӮӮз§°гҒ•гӮҢгӮӢгҖӮ</ref>гӮ’дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ гҒ®зҘ–гҒЁгҒ—гҒҰгҒ„гӮӢгҖӮгҖҺ[[еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ]]гҖҸгҒ«гӮҲгӮӢгҒЁ[[жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ]]гҒ®жҷӮд»ЈгҒ«еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘ҪгҒ®е…«дё–еӯ«гҒ®[[еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ]]гӮ’еӣҪйҖ гҒ«е®ҡгӮҒгҒҹгҒЁгҒ•гӮҢгҖҒгҒқгҒ®еӯ«[[д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ]]гҒҢ[[еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ]]гҒ®жҷӮд»ЈгҒ«дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ гҒ«гҒӘгҒЈгҒҹгҒЁгҒ„гҒҶгҖӮ[[еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ ]]гғ»[[жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ |ж— йӮӘеҝ—еӣҪйҖ ]]гғ»[[дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ ]]гғ»[[дјҠз”ҡеӣҪйҖ |дјҠиҮӘзүҹеӣҪйҖ ]]гғ»[[йҒ ж·Ўжө·еӣҪйҖ ]]гҒӘгҒ©гҒЁеҗҢзі»гҒ®[[еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ |еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ]]гҒҢеӣҪйҖ гӮ’дё–иҘІгҒ—гҒҹгҒЁиҖғгҒҲгӮүгӮҢгҒҰгҒ„гӮӢгҖӮгҖҺ[[з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ]]гҖҸгҒ«гҒҜгҖҒзҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІе…ғе№ҙ9жңҲ22ж—ҘжқЎгҒ«гҖҢдёҠз·ҸеӣҪжө·дёҠйғЎдәәеӨ–еҫ“дә”дҪҚдёӢгҒ®[[жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәзӣҙе»әйә»е‘Ӯ]]гҒ«дёҠз·Ҹе®ҝзҰ°гҒ®е§“гӮ’иіңгҒөгҖҚгҒЁгҖҒгҒҫгҒҹе®қдәҖ6е№ҙ3жңҲ2ж—ҘжқЎгҒ«гҖҢеӨ–еҫ“дә”дҪҚдёӢгҒ®дёҠз·Ҹе®ҝзҰ°е»әйә»е‘ӮгӮ’йҡјдәәжӯЈгҒЁзӮәгҒҷгҖҚгҒЁгҒӮгӮҠгҖҒдёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ гҒ®еӯҗеӯ«гҒЁгҒҝгӮүгӮҢ[[дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ]]гҒ®зҘ–гҒЁгӮӮгҒ•гӮҢгӮӢгҖӮ
гҒӘгҒҠгҖҒ[[е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ]]гҒ«гҒҜдёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ гҒ«д»»гҒҳгӮүгӮҢгҒҹеҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘ҪгҒҢеӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘ҪгҒЁеЎһдёүжҹұзҘһгӮ’зҘҖгӮҠгҖҒеҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘ҪгҒ®еӯ«гғ»еҝҚе…је‘ҪгҒҢ[[д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ|еӨ§йӣҖе‘Ҫ]]гӮ’еҗҲзҘҖгҒ—гҒҹгҒЁгҒ•гӮҢгҒҰгҒҠгӮҠгҖҒе§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨгҒҜе§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫгӮ’[[ж°ҸзҘһ]]гҒЁгҒ—гҒҰзҘҖгҒЈгҒҰгҒ„гҒҹдёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ дёҖж—ҸгҒ®еўіеў“гҒЁгҒ„гӮҸгӮҢгҒҰгҒ„гӮӢгҖӮ
== и„ҡжіЁ == {{Reflist}}
== еҸӮиҖғж–ҮзҢ® == *гҖҺеңӢеҸІеӨ§иҫӯе…ёгҖҸпјҲ[[еҗүе·қејҳж–ҮйӨЁ]]пјү *еқӮжң¬еӨӘйғҺгғ»е№ійҮҺйӮҰйӣ„гҖҺж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨд»Јж°Ҹж—ҸдәәеҗҚиҫһе…ёгҖҸпјҲеҗүе·қејҳж–ҮйӨЁпјү *гҖҺж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨд»ЈдәәеҗҚиҫӯе…ёгҖҸпјҲеҗүе·қејҳж–ҮйӨЁпјү *гҖҺж—Ҙжң¬еҸІеәғиҫһе…ёгҖҸпјҲ[[еұұе·қеҮәзүҲзӨҫ]]пјү *гҖҺзҘһйҒ“еӨ§иҫһе…ёгҖҸпјҲиҮЁе·қжӣёеә—пјү *гҖҺж—Ҙжң¬еҸІз·ҸиҰ§гҖҸпјҲ[[ж–°дәәзү©еҫҖжқҘзӨҫ]]пјү *[[е®қиіҖеҜҝз”·]]гҖҺи¶ҠгҒЁеҮәйӣІгҒ®еӨңжҳҺгҒ‘гҖҸпјҲжі•д»ӨеҮәзүҲпјү2008е№ҙ
== й–ўйҖЈй …зӣ® == *[[еӣҪйҖ гҒ®дёҖиҰ§]]
== еӨ–йғЁгғӘгғігӮҜ == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/kamitu.html жҲҝз·ҸгҒ®еҸӨеўігӮ’жӯ©гҒҸ дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪгҒ®еҸӨеўі]

revision.text:
'''дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ '''пјҲгҒӢгҒҝгҒӨгҒҶгҒӘгҒӢгҒҝгҒ®гҒҸгҒ«гҒҝгӮ„гҒӨгҒ“гғ»гҒӢгҒҝгҒӨгҒҶгҒӘгҒӢгҒҝгҒ“гҒҸгҒһгҒҶпјүгҒҜгҖҒ[[дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ]][[жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)|жө·дёҠйғЎ]]пјҲзҸҫеңЁгҒ®[[еҚғи‘үзңҢ]][[еёӮеҺҹеёӮ]]гҒ®дёҖйғЁпјүгӮ’ж”Ҝй…ҚгҒ—гҒҹ[[еӣҪйҖ ]]гҖӮдёҠиҸҹдёҠеӣҪйҖ гҒЁгӮӮгҖӮ
== жҰӮиҰҒ == й ҳеҹҹгҒ®[[йӨҠиҖҒе·қ]]жөҒеҹҹгҒ«гҒҜгҖҒ[[3дё–зҙҖ]]гҒ®[[еҮәзҸҫжңҹеҸӨеўі]]пјҲ[[зә’еҗ‘еһӢеүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі]]пјүгҒ§гҒӮгӮӢ[[зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ]]<ref>гҒ“гӮҢгӮ’еҸӨеўігҒЁгҒҷгӮӢгҒЁ[[еҸӨеўіжҷӮд»Ј]]гҒ®е§ӢгҒҫгӮҠгҒҢеӨ§е№…гҒ«гҒ•гҒӢгҒ®гҒјгӮӢгҒҹгӮҒгҖҒеҸӨеўігҒ§гҒҜгҒӘгҒҸ[[ејҘз”ҹеўідёҳеў“]]гҒЁгҒҷгӮӢиӘ¬гӮӮгҒӮгӮӢгҖӮ</ref>гӮ„гҖҒ[[йү„еүЈгғ»йү„еҲҖйҠҳж–Ү|зҺӢиіңйҠҳ]]йү„еүЈгӮ’еҮәеңҹгҒ—гҒҹ[[зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі]]<ref>гҒӘгҒҠгҖҒгҒ“гӮҢгӮүйӨҠиҖҒе·қеҢ—еІёгҒ®еҸӨеўізҫӨгҒҜйҰ–й•·гҒ®еўіеў“гҒ§гҒҜгҒӘгҒҸе°ҸеӢўеҠӣгҒ®гӮӮгҒ®гҒ§гҖҒгҒ“гҒ®гҒҹгӮҒзҺӢиіңйҠҳйү„еүЈгҒ®гҖҢзҺӢгҖҚгӮ’гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©гҒ®[[жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ|еӨ§зҺӢ]]гҒЁгҒҷгӮӢд»–гҖҒдёҠжө·дёҠгҒ®йҰ–й•·гҒ§гҒӮгӮӢеҜҫеІёгҒ®е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўігҒ®иў«и‘¬иҖ…гҒЁгҒҝгӮӢиӘ¬гӮӮгҒӮгӮӢгҖӮ</ref>гҒҢгҒӮгӮҠгҖҒеҸӨгҒҸгҒӢгӮү[[гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©]]гҒЁз·ҠеҜҶгҒӘгҒӨгҒӘгҒҢгӮҠгҒҢгҒӮгҒЈгҒҹгҒ“гҒЁгҒҢжғіе®ҡгҒ•гӮҢгӮӢгҖӮгҖҺ[[дёҮи‘үйӣҶ]]гҖҸе·»з¬¬еҚҒеӣӣ[[жқұжӯҢ (е’ҢжӯҢ)|жқұжӯҢ]]гҒ®зӯҶй ӯгҒ«гҖҢеӨҸйә»еј•гҒҸжө·дёҠжҪҹгҒ®жІ–гҒӨжёҡгҒ«иҲ№гҒҜгҒЁгҒ©гӮҒгӮҖе°ҸеӨңгҒөгҒ‘гҒ«гҒ‘гӮҠгҖҚгҒЁгҒӮгӮҠгҖҒ[[ж°ҙдёҠдәӨйҖҡ]]гҒ®иҰҒиЎқгҒ§гҒӮгҒЈгҒҹгҒЁгӮӮгҒ•гӮҢгӮӢгҖӮ
гҒҫгҒҹгҖҒеҪ“еӣҪйҖ гҒ®й ҳеҹҹгҒЁ[[дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ ]]гҒ®й ҳеҹҹгҒ®й–“гҒ«[[жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ ]]гҒ®й ҳеҹҹгҒҢгҒҜгҒ„гҒЈгҒҰгҒ„гӮӢгҒ“гҒЁгҒӢгӮүгҖҒжҲҝз·ҸгҒ®еӣҪйҖ еҲ¶гҒ®еұ•й–ӢгҒ«гҒҜе°‘гҒӘгҒҸгҒЁгӮӮ2ж®өйҡҺгҒҢгҒӮгҒЈгҒҹгҒЁгҒ•гӮҢгҖҒ[[5дё–зҙҖ]]гҒҫгҒ§гҒҜдёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪгӮӮдҪөгҒӣгҖҒеҚғи‘үзңҢдёӯйғЁгҒӢгӮү[[иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ]]гҖҒ[[еҹјзҺүзңҢ]]гҖҒ[[жқұдә¬йғҪ]]гҒ«гҒӢгҒ‘гҒҰгҒ®дёҖеёҜгӮ’ж”Ҝй…ҚгҒ—гҒҹеӨ§еӢўеҠӣгҒ§гҒӮгҒЈгҒҹгҒЁгҒҷгӮӢиӘ¬гӮӮгҒӮгӮӢгҖӮ
гҒқгҒ®еҫҢ[[6дё–зҙҖ]]гҒ«дёӯеӨ®гҒӢгӮүйҖІеҮәгҒ—гҒҹеӢўеҠӣгҒ®е»әгҒҰгҒҹжӯҰзӨҫеӣҪгҒ«гӮҲгҒЈгҒҰдёҠгғ»дёӢгҒ«еҲҶеүІгҒ•гӮҢгҖҒгҒ•гӮүгҒ«[[жқ‘з”°е·қ]]жөҒеҹҹгҒ®[[иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ ]]гҒ®еҸ°й ӯгҒ«гӮҲгӮҠиЎ°йҖҖгӮ’дҪҷе„ҖгҒӘгҒҸгҒ•гӮҢгҒҹгҖӮйӨҠиҖҒе·қдёӢжөҒеҚ—еІёгҒ®[[е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ]]гҒҜеҪ“еӣҪйҖ дёҖж—ҸгҒ®еўіеў“гҒЁгҒ•гӮҢгҖҒ[[4дё–зҙҖ]]еҫҢеҚҠгҒ®е…Ёй•·130гғЎгғјгғҲгғ«гҒ®[[е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі]]гҖҒ5дё–зҙҖеүҚеҚҠгҒ®е…Ёй•·86гғЎгғјгғҲгғ«гҒ®йҮҲиҝҰеұұеҸӨеўігҒ«з¶ҡгҒҚгҖҒ5дё–зҙҖеҫҢеҚҠгҒ«гҒҜе…Ёй•·103гғЎгғјгғҲгғ«гҒ®[[е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі]]гҒҢйҖ е–¶гҒ•гӮҢгҒҰгҒ„гӮӢгҒҢгҖҒ6дё–зҙҖд»ҘйҷҚгҒЁгҒ•гӮҢгӮӢеҸӨеўігҒҢеҫҗгҖ…гҒ«зё®е°ҸгҒ—гҒҰгҒ„гӮӢгҒ“гҒЁгҒӢгӮүгӮӮиЎ°йҖҖгҒҢиЈҸд»ҳгҒ‘гӮүгӮҢгӮӢгҖӮ
=== ж°Ҹж—Ҹ === гҖҺ[[еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ]]гҖҸгҒ§гҒҜ[[гӮўгғЎгғҺгғӣгғ’|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ]]гҒ®еӯҗгҒ®[[е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ]]<ref>е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘ҪгҒҜгҖҒжӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫгғ»еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫгғ»еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘ҪгҒӘгҒ©гҒЁгӮӮз§°гҒ•гӮҢгӮӢгҖӮ</ref>гӮ’дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ гҒ®зҘ–гҒЁгҒ—гҒҰгҒ„гӮӢгҖӮгҖҺ[[еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ]]гҖҸгҒ«гӮҲгӮӢгҒЁ[[жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ]]гҒ®жҷӮд»ЈгҒ«еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘ҪгҒ®е…«дё–еӯ«гҒ®[[еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ]]гӮ’еӣҪйҖ гҒ«е®ҡгӮҒгҒҹгҒЁгҒ•гӮҢгҖҒгҒқгҒ®еӯ«[[д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ]]гҒҢ[[еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ]]гҒ®жҷӮд»ЈгҒ«дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ гҒ«гҒӘгҒЈгҒҹгҒЁгҒ„гҒҶгҖӮ[[еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ ]]гғ»[[жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ |ж— йӮӘеҝ—еӣҪйҖ ]]гғ»[[дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ ]]гғ»[[дјҠз”ҡеӣҪйҖ |дјҠиҮӘзүҹеӣҪйҖ ]]гғ»[[йҒ ж·Ўжө·еӣҪйҖ ]]гҒӘгҒ©гҒЁеҗҢзі»гҒ®[[еҮәйӣІеӣҪйҖ |еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ]]гҒҢеӣҪйҖ гӮ’дё–иҘІгҒ—гҒҹгҒЁиҖғгҒҲгӮүгӮҢгҒҰгҒ„гӮӢгҖӮгҖҺ[[з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ]]гҖҸгҒ«гҒҜгҖҒзҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІе…ғе№ҙ9жңҲ22ж—ҘжқЎгҒ«гҖҢдёҠз·ҸеӣҪжө·дёҠйғЎдәәеӨ–еҫ“дә”дҪҚдёӢгҒ®[[жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәзӣҙе»әйә»е‘Ӯ]]гҒ«дёҠз·Ҹе®ҝзҰ°гҒ®е§“гӮ’иіңгҒөгҖҚгҒЁгҖҒгҒҫгҒҹе®қдәҖ6е№ҙ3жңҲ2ж—ҘжқЎгҒ«гҖҢеӨ–еҫ“дә”дҪҚдёӢгҒ®дёҠз·Ҹе®ҝзҰ°е»әйә»е‘ӮгӮ’йҡјдәәжӯЈгҒЁзӮәгҒҷгҖҚгҒЁгҒӮгӮҠгҖҒдёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ гҒ®еӯҗеӯ«гҒЁгҒҝгӮүгӮҢ[[дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ]]гҒ®зҘ–гҒЁгӮӮгҒ•гӮҢгӮӢгҖӮ
гҒӘгҒҠгҖҒ[[е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ]]гҒ«гҒҜдёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ гҒ«д»»гҒҳгӮүгӮҢгҒҹеҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘ҪгҒҢеӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘ҪгҒЁеЎһдёүжҹұзҘһгӮ’зҘҖгӮҠгҖҒеҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘ҪгҒ®еӯ«гғ»еҝҚе…је‘ҪгҒҢ[[д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ|еӨ§йӣҖе‘Ҫ]]гӮ’еҗҲзҘҖгҒ—гҒҹгҒЁгҒ•гӮҢгҒҰгҒҠгӮҠгҖҒе§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨгҒҜе§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫгӮ’[[ж°ҸзҘһ]]гҒЁгҒ—гҒҰзҘҖгҒЈгҒҰгҒ„гҒҹдёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ дёҖж—ҸгҒ®еўіеў“гҒЁгҒ„гӮҸгӮҢгҒҰгҒ„гӮӢгҖӮ
== и„ҡжіЁ == {{Reflist}}
== еҸӮиҖғж–ҮзҢ® == *гҖҺеңӢеҸІеӨ§иҫӯе…ёгҖҸпјҲ[[еҗүе·қејҳж–ҮйӨЁ]]пјү *еқӮжң¬еӨӘйғҺгғ»е№ійҮҺйӮҰйӣ„гҖҺж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨд»Јж°Ҹж—ҸдәәеҗҚиҫһе…ёгҖҸпјҲеҗүе·қејҳж–ҮйӨЁпјү *гҖҺж—Ҙжң¬еҸӨд»ЈдәәеҗҚиҫӯе…ёгҖҸпјҲеҗүе·қејҳж–ҮйӨЁпјү *гҖҺж—Ҙжң¬еҸІеәғиҫһе…ёгҖҸпјҲ[[еұұе·қеҮәзүҲзӨҫ]]пјү *гҖҺзҘһйҒ“еӨ§иҫһе…ёгҖҸпјҲиҮЁе·қжӣёеә—пјү *гҖҺж—Ҙжң¬еҸІз·ҸиҰ§гҖҸпјҲ[[ж–°дәәзү©еҫҖжқҘзӨҫ]]пјү *[[е®қиіҖеҜҝз”·]]гҖҺи¶ҠгҒЁеҮәйӣІгҒ®еӨңжҳҺгҒ‘гҖҸпјҲжі•д»ӨеҮәзүҲпјү2008е№ҙ
== й–ўйҖЈй …зӣ® == *[[еӣҪйҖ гҒ®дёҖиҰ§]]
== еӨ–йғЁгғӘгғігӮҜ == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/kamitu.html жҲҝз·ҸгҒ®еҸӨеўігӮ’жӯ©гҒҸ дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪгҒ®еҸӨеўі]
{{DEFAULTSORT:гҒӢгҒҝгҒӨгҒҶгҒӘгҒӢгҒҝгҒ®гҒҸгҒ«гҒ®гҒҝгӮ„гҒӨгҒ“}} [[Category:ж—Ҙжң¬гҒ®жӯҙеҸІй–ўйҖЈгҒ®гӮ№гӮҝгғ–й …зӣ®]] [[Category:дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ]] [[Category:еӣҪйҖ ]] [[Category:еҚғи‘үзңҢгҒ®жӯҙеҸІ]]

'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ , also known as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ) governed the {{ill|KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|ja|жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which corresponds to part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј|qq=draft|qq=draft}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inaridai No.1 Kofun|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі|Inaridai No.1 Kofun=Inaridai No.1 Kofun|lt=Inaritai No.1 Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In addition, the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ |qq=draft|qq=draft}} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ |qq=draft|qq=draft}} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ |qq=draft|qq=draft}} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Kofun (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ|1=Takehi-Nateru|1=Takehi-Nateru|zh|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ|1=Takehi-Nateru|fr|qq=draft|qq=draft}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|Izumo Province йҖ |qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ |qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°|1=Sukune|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ|1=Ame-no-Koyane|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Kofun Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.

[[sn:Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko]] [[en:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] [[sn:Shanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] [[ja:дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ ]] [[sn:hanghai Kamitsu no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]

revision.comment: Bot: add simplewiki draft link to ill templates
revision.text:
'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ , also known as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ) governed the {{ill|KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|ja|жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|qq=}} in {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazusa Province|qq=}}, which corresponds to part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichihara|qq=}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiba Prefecture|qq=}}. It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/YЕҚrЕҚ River|qq=}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamado Kofun Cluster|qq=}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi period|qq=}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inaridai No.1 Kofun|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі|Inaridai No.1 Kofun=Inaridai No.1 Kofun|lt=Inaritai No.1 Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaridai No.1 Kofun|qq=}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ЕҢkimi|qq=}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anegasaki Twin Mounds|13=User:Immanuelle/Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|qq=}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Court|qq=}}. In addition, the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ManyЕҚshЕ«|qq=}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ |qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ |qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ibaraki Prefecture|qq=}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitama Prefecture|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo|qq=}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ |qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murata River|qq=}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|qq=}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anegasaki Tenjinyama Kofun|qq=}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Kofun (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hohi|qq=}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ|1=Takehi-Nateru|1=Takehi-Nateru|zh|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ|1=Takehi-Nateru|fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehiratori no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|qq=}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/KokuzЕҚ Hongi|qq=}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seimu|qq=}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|qq=}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hisatsuki no Atai|qq=}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ЕҢjin|qq=}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo clan|qq=}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|Izumo Province йҖ |qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|qq=}} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ |qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|qq=}}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoku Nihongi|qq=}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingo Keiun|qq=}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himuro Tokunaga|qq=}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°|1=Sukune|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/е®ҝзҰ°|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukune|qq=}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/HЕҚki|qq=}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazusa clan|qq=}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anesaki Shrine|qq=}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ|1=Ame-no-Koyane|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane|qq=}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nintoku|qq=}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Kofun Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujigami|qq=}}.

In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ |qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murata River|qq=}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|qq=}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anegasaki Tenjinyama Kofun|qq=}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Kofun (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hohi|qq=}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ|1=Takehi-Nateru|1=Takehi-Nateru|zh|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ|1=Takehi-Nateru|fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehiratori no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|qq=}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/KokuzЕҚ Hongi|qq=}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seimu|qq=}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|qq=}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hisatsuki no Atai|qq=}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ЕҢjin|qq=}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo clan|qq=}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|Izumo Province йҖ |qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|qq=}} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ |qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|qq=}}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoku Nihongi|qq=}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingo Keiun|qq=}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himuro Tokunaga|qq=}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°|1=Sukune|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/е®ҝзҰ°|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukune|qq=}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/HЕҚki|qq=}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazusa clan|qq=}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anesaki Shrine|qq=}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ|1=Ame-no-Koyane|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane|qq=}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nintoku|qq=}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Kofun Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujigami|qq=}}.

revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Kamado Kofun Cluster]] вҶ’ [[GЕҚdo Kofun Cluster]]
revision.text:
'''Kamitsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko''' (дёҠжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ , also known as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ) governed the {{ill|KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|ja|жө·дёҠйғЎ (дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/KaijЕҚ District, Kazusa Province|qq=}} in {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|дёҠз·ҸеӣҪ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazusa Province|qq=}}, which corresponds to part of present-day {{ill|Ichihara|ja|еёӮеҺҹеёӮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichihara|qq=}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|еҚғи‘үзңҢ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiba Prefecture|qq=}}. It is also referred to as Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ.
== Overview == The region surrounding the {{ill|YЕҚrЕҚ River|ja|йӨҠиҖҒе·қ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/YЕҚrЕҚ River|qq=}} basin features archaeological sites such as the {{ill|GЕҚdo Kofun Cluster|lt=GЕҚdo Kofun Cluster|lt=Kamado Kofun Cluster|ja|зҘһй–ҖеҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamado Kofun Cluster|qq=}}, believed to be early 3rd-century keyhole-shaped tombs ({{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚen-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}}),<ref>This interpretation as kofun shifts the beginning of the Kofun period significantly earlier. Others suggest these are late {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|ејҘз”ҹжҷӮд»Ј|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi period|qq=}} mound burials.</ref> and the {{ill|Inaridai No.1 Kofun|ja|зЁІиҚ·еҸ°1еҸ·еҸӨеўі|Inaridai No.1 Kofun=Inaridai No.1 Kofun|lt=Inaritai No.1 Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaridai No.1 Kofun|qq=}}, which yielded an iron sword inscribed with the royal title {{ill|ЕҢkimi|ja|жІ»еӨ©дёӢеӨ§зҺӢ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ЕҢkimi|qq=}}.<ref>While some scholars attribute the title to the Yamato court, others link it to the chief buried in the nearby {{ill|Anegasaki Twin Mounds|ja|е§үеҙҺдәҢеӯҗеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anegasaki Twin Mounds|13=User:Immanuelle/Anesaki Futagozuka Kofun|qq=}}.</ref> These indicate close ties with the {{ill|Yamato Court|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Court|qq=}}. In addition, the "{{ill|ManyЕҚshЕ«|ja|дёҮи‘үйӣҶ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ManyЕҚshЕ«|qq=}}" (Volume 14) includes an Eastern song that begins with "Natsuma pulls at the seacoast of KaijЕҚ Lagoon, the boat lingers on the shore as night deepens," suggesting its importance as a hub for waterway traffic.
The placement of {{ill|Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|жӯҰзӨҫеӣҪйҖ |qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}} territory between Kamitsu Unakami and {{ill|Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|дёӢжө·дёҠеӣҪйҖ |qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimotsu Unakami no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}} regions implies that the establishment of KokuzЕҚ governance in the BЕҚsЕҚ Peninsula occurred in stages. By the 5th century, the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ collectively exerted influence from central Chiba through parts of modern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|иҢЁеҹҺзңҢ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ibaraki Prefecture|qq=}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|еҹјзҺүзңҢ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitama Prefecture|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|жқұдә¬йғҪ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo|qq=}}, a domain sometimes called "Greater Unakami Kuni."
In the 6th century, emerging central forces established Musha no Kuni Miyatsuko, dividing Kamitsu and Shimotsu Unakami regions. Further decline followed the rise of the {{ill|Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|ja|иҸҠйә»еӣҪйҖ |qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikuma no Kuni Miyatsuko|qq=}} along the {{ill|Murata River|ja|жқ‘з”°е·қ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murata River|qq=}}. The {{ill|Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|ja|е§үеҙҺеҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anegasaki Kofun Cluster|qq=}} is considered the burial site of the Kamitsu Unakami lineage, featuring notable tumuli such as the 130-meter-long {{ill|Anegasaki Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|е§үеҙҺеӨ©зҘһеұұеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anegasaki Tenjinyama Kofun|qq=}} (late 4th century), the 86-meter-long Shakazan Kofun (early 5th century), and the 103-meter-long Anegasaki Twin Mounds (late 5th century). Shrinking mound sizes in subsequent centuries reflect the lineage's declining power.
=== Lineage === According to the "{{ill|Kojiki|ja|еҸӨдәӢиЁҳ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}}," {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|еӨ©з©Ӯж—Ҙе‘Ҫ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hohi|qq=}}вҖҷs son, {{ill|Takehiratori no Mikoto|ja|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ|1=Takehi-Nateru|1=Takehi-Nateru|zh|е»әжҜ”иүҜйіҘе‘Ҫ|1=Takehi-Nateru|fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehiratori no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehi-Nateru|qq=}},<ref>Takehiratori no Mikoto is also called жӯҰеӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, еӨ©еӨ·йіҘе‘Ҫ, or еӨ©ж—Ҙз…§е‘Ҫ in other sources.</ref> was the progenitor of Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ. The "{{ill|KokuzЕҚ Hongi|ja|еӣҪйҖ жң¬зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/KokuzЕҚ Hongi|qq=}}" states that during {{ill|Seimu|ja|жҲҗеӢҷеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seimu|qq=}}вҖҷs reign, Ame-no-Hohi's eighth-generation descendant, {{ill|Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|ja|еҝҚз«ӢеҢ–еӨҡжҜ”е‘Ҫ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto|qq=}}, was appointed as KokuzЕҚ. His grandson {{ill|Hisatsuki no Atai|ja|д№…йғҪдјҺзӣҙ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hisatsuki no Atai|qq=}} later became the Shimotsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ under Emperor {{ill|ЕҢjin|ja|еҝңзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ЕҢjin|qq=}}. The Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ lineage, linked to the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|еҮәйӣІж°Ҹ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo clan|qq=}}, shared ancestry with other KokuzЕҚ, such as the {{ill|Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|Izumo Province йҖ |qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province KokuzЕҚ|qq=}} and {{ill|Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|ja|жӯҰи”өеӣҪйҖ |qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musashi Province KokuzЕҚ|qq=}}.
The "{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|з¶ҡж—Ҙжң¬зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoku Nihongi|qq=}}" (Shoku Nihongi) mentions that in 767 ({{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|зҘһиӯ·жҷҜйӣІ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingo Keiun|qq=}} 1), {{ill|Himuro Tokunaga|ja|жӘңеүҚиҲҺдәәе»әйә»е‘Ӯ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himuro Tokunaga|qq=}} of KaijЕҚ District was granted the surname "Kazusa Sukune" ({{ill|е®ҝзҰ°|ja|е®ҝзҰ°|1=Sukune|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/е®ҝзҰ°|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukune|qq=}}). Another entry in 775 ({{ill|HЕҚki|ja|е®қдәҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/HЕҚki|qq=}} 6) notes that he was appointed to an official post in Satsuma, suggesting that he may have been a descendant of the Kamitsu Unakami KokuzЕҚ and an ancestor of the {{ill|Kazusa clan|ja|дёҠз·Ҹж°Ҹ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazusa clan|qq=}}.
The {{ill|Anesaki Shrine|ja|е§үеҹјзҘһзӨҫ|sv|Anesaki-jinja|sv=Anesaki|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anesaki Shrine|qq=}} is said to have been established by Oshitatetakahitome no Mikoto to enshrine the deities {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|ja|еӨ©е…җеұӢж №е‘Ҫ|1=Ame-no-Koyane|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane|qq=}} and three protective deities. His grandson Oshikane no Mikoto later added {{ill|Nintoku|ja|д»ҒеҫіеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nintoku|qq=}} to the enshrined deities. The Anegasaki Kofun Cluster is believed to include burial sites for this KokuzЕҚ lineage, which worshiped Ane Saki Shrine as their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|ж°ҸзҘһ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujigami|qq=}}.
